old_claim.note: Charterparty Shipping Contracts provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Medieval port cranes and crane-gates were built structures with masonry, timber, and lifting mechanisms, so construction capability is the direct physical enabler.

source_shape.source_locator: History section describes Gdańsk Żuraw as a medieval port crane-gate rebuilt in 1442-1444, with masonry construction, lifting drums, treadwheels, and workers operating the mechanism.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports port crane-gate infrastructure as a constructed harbor machine and building, not as a direct product of shipping-contract law.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Construction is required for the scoped physical crane, quay, and crane-gate infrastructure. Charterparty shipping contracts may organize cargo carriage, but they do not physically enable the harbor crane works represented by this node.

ontology_before: Port Cranes & Harbor Works depended on Charterparty Shipping Contracts, implying physical harbor lifting infrastructure required written ship-hire contracts as a direct prerequisite.
ontology_after: Port Cranes & Harbor Works depends on Construction and Guilds, preserving physical infrastructure and organized labor/trade context while removing the contract-law prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: charterparty_shipping_contracts is removed and replaced with construction. Preserved: the node remains physical medieval harbor infrastructure, not maritime documents or navigation practice.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to contractual port-administration systems rather than physical harbor crane works.
A source shows charterparty contracts were a direct prerequisite for constructing or operating medieval harbor cranes.
A narrower medieval_harbor_cranes node is introduced and this broad node is reserved for administrative port services.
short_reason: Source-backed harbor cranes are physical constructed infrastructure, not a direct output of charterparty contracts.
